comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: You've mentioned before that the subconscious is good at creating fake past life memories. How do you distinguish those from real ones?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
First, don't go looking for them -- that's the best way to get fabrications. Second, watch for wish-fulfillment fantasies and other forms of ego stroking, which are omnipresent in fabricated memories -- that is to say, you weren't Cleopatra; deal. Third, when apparent memories surface, get as many details of the setting as you can -- exactly what were you wearing, and how did it fasten? How was the food served? Little things like that can then be looked up, and if you find that details you didn't know check out, you may well have something real.

The most useful touchstone for recent past lives, though, is that they explain things in this life. Patterns continue from life to life, and as the memories start to surface, if they're real, you'll find yourself over and over again thinking, "Oh, that's why I was so scared of heights when I was small!" and things like that.

A lot of the things I seem to remember from that life make a great deal of sense of some otherwise odd things about me. With this past life I found myself going "oh, that's why..." many, many times.

It's also combined with having a handful of memories from the life right before and right after, with that last one being right before mine. The few memories I have all trigger "so that's why..." reactions as well, and also seem far more mundane, so maybe it's just I had one interesting life?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Most people have had minor leadership positions at some point in their lives, and the headship of a Druid order is actually a very minor thing -- trust me, I know! ;-) All through the history of the Revival, Druid organizations have tended to be modest in size and influence, so it's not much of an ego stroke to say, "I was an archdruid in 1870," or what have you; so were a lot of other people.

comments.title: Astral travelling baby
comments.content: Have you ever heard of very young children astral travelling? In the first few weeks of my son's life, my wife and I would wake in the night regularly thinking that he was in the bed with us, sometimes frantically searching through the bedclothes until we woke up enough to realise he was in his bassinet. I remember quite clearly the impression of a 1-foot-longish ovoid presence laying in between us. There was also the time that something knocked a couple of times on the bedside table to wake me when he had a really dirty nappy and had thrown up on himself but was sleeping through it. That's probably another phenomenon entirely though! He's six weeks old now and this stuff has settled down now.

replies.title: Re: Astral travelling baby
replies.content:
Happens all the time. Infants aren't fully anchored into the physical body for the first year and a half or so of life, and if your son did any significant amount of spiritual practice or occult training in his last life, he could well have carried the abilities over with him.

If he shows other signs of being loosely linked to the physical -- "imaginary" (i.e., nonphysical) friends, precognitive dreams, past life memories, etc. -- you'll want to make sure, without discouraging these things, that he gets a lot of exposure to the purely physical side of existence as well; as he gets older, try to get him interested in nature, and in whatever kinds of physical activity he likes and can handle; that way he'll have a solid grounding in the material plane once puberty hits and the subtle energy channels start opening up.

comments.title: Fictional sources

JMG

Firstly, please allow me to apologise for trying to sneak this question through late last week. It was terribly rude of me, and I don't know what I was thinking.

Anyway, last week you said in response to another question that "if you base your magic on a fictional source, you'll get fictional results", and I was wondering if this applies as much to using a fictional pantheon with otherwise established magical practices as it does to fictional practices. My specific example was using Tolkein's Valar in your pantheistic version of the LBRP...

replies.title: Re: Fictional sources
replies.content:
People have been doing that sort of thing since the 1970s, with fairly poor results. You can get some effect from fictional beings, but it's purely the effect you get from using an imagined figure as a focus for your concentration and intent -- basically, the Chaos Magic approach. If you want to get the benefits of interacting with a real being, you need to invoke one.

Let me ask you this. If you were suddenly to find yourself in a situation where your life was at risk, in all seriousness, would you call on Elbereth and expect to get a response? If not, I don't advise invoking her in magic, either.

(Oh, and I posted a polytheist version of the LBRP, not a pantheist version. There's a major difference!)

comments.title: Post part 1: In good news I found a patron (Freyja)
comments.content:
This will be longish so i'll break it into 2 posts...

In good news, I took your advice as a "godless" and called out to find a patron, Freyja responded strongly.

Background: After a banishing ritual, one of the meditative breathing exercises in your learning ritual magic book, I called out and asked for a patron to guide me on the secret path. While meditating nothing initially happened then the image came to me of one of the areas that had drawn my eyes in my tarot deck, in the thoth deck just over the emperor's shoulder there's a hill flanked by rams under a sun. First I was on the hill surrounded by waist high wheat, then I felt a presence nearby hidden in it, I felt afraid because it was alien but not that it was hostile. I accepted the presence/imagery and continued on instead of withdrawing or shutting down. The scene transitioned and I was in front of the tower briefly, black stone with steam or mist around it. The scene shifted again and I was facing a pitch black lake in a pitch black night with a huge full moon reflected overhead. I realized i was being guided through the tarot cards I had drawn that day.

(Used the draw pattern in the thoth LWB, 14 cards, had hermit, prince of wands,2 wands in querent. the moon, ace of wands, 7 disks failure in path top left, emperor, empress, and chariot bottom left for psych state, lust, queen of wands, princess of cups bottom right for destiny/karma, and 4 disks power, 3 swords sorrow, queen of swords top right for other path)

In any event, i stood at the edge of the water and then went down into it and floated down on my back into the dark with the moon receded on the surface of the water above me. I floated down until I hit mud at bottom and just lie there, perfect black above and below. The scene shifted and I was now in space surrounded by blackness, a white door appeared with a gold know (comically it looked like the scene out of the old black and white twilight zone opening when the door appears) I went through the door and down a tunnel of white/blue light. I felt I had the symbols to go on to find my patron, norse/germanic/northern europe, the wheat field, the white rose, a female deity, and one with a nooky orientation. A quick google search later and Freyja was the first hit and it felt "right". I had a lingering feeling of peace and calm in my heart for days afterward. (the previous time I'd felt that peace was after converting to christianity as a teen in a group revival session)

I had no previous knowledge of her, her background, dominions etc. I was very happy with the fit though. I don't feel drawn to the male norse gods at all with the exception of Baldr, possibly Rigr, and a few others. So a female patron felt right and over the next few days I felt a lot of my remaining christian baggage fall away along with my guilt for having been in the military.

comments.title: post part 2: in bad news, i asked to be ridden by Freyja via Seidh trance, it was terrifying
comments.content:
Here's post part 2, the bad news, and the real reason why I'm writing all of this for your advice.

I did something very foolish, based on my divination Sunday with the fool, fortune, priestess and other cards. I was emboldened after my patron vision and wanted to see if I would be shown anything further. After reading about freyja at various sites and specifically the seidh possesion trance here via Ember Cooke. http://www.northernpaganism.org/shrines/freya/praising-freya/freya-speaks.html

(unfortunately I didn't find her OTHER site dealing with the ins and outs of possesion trance here https://embervoices.wordpress.com/2014/05/12/possession-trance-experience-spectrum/ until later...)

Short version, I asked freyja to enter and ride me, she came via some specific imagery, and I had the first dissociation experience I've ever had sober (the only other time was while using pot as a teen 3 decades ago) I did not like the feeling then and I most assuredly didn't like the feeling now with the feeling that someone was inside me looking through my eyes. It was the single most terrifying experience I've ever had. I panicked, nearly threw up, conducted a quick banishing ritual, wound up on the bathroom floor, at one point was doing quick chopping motions with my hand parallel to my chest while saying "i sever the link" no idea where that came from, and started forcefully saying leave me, my body is my own, get out, etc.This sort of thing continued for a bit until the dissociation passed.

I now feel completely shell shocked, stunned, and worried. Did i invite a demon in? Will there be lasting damage? Or is the magical equivalent of singeing your face and fingers?

After finding and reading Ember Cooke's trance possession writing I feel slightly better as my experience meshes with what she writes so I feel it was more a freyja experience as her specific imagery was involved as she arrived as opposed to your description of shells. While being ridden I felt calm, interacted with my family normally, could tell them things like i loved them and such, so again i'd think with a shell it would have had a different "taste." The terror didn't start until i panicked at the growing dissociation and tried to forcefully end it. Using Cooke's rubric I was only at about 10-15% possession, enough to feel presence and feel detached but no voices and such, though there was a brief almost heard voice while I forcefully tried to sever contact. (after re-reading her Freyja speaks bit about the fear about being ridden that's almost exactly what i felt and panicked)

So lessons learned:
- no more wandering off via intuition, i'm going to stick to the training regiment you recommend in learning ritual magic. i was NOT prepared for that experience.
- magic is serious business. As a rational, high analytic who up till 6 months ago didn't believe in spiritual mumbo jumbo I did NOT take this seriously enough. Likewise i'm just stunned at the breadth and depth of spiritual power and powers.
- your patron and the pantheon are not human friends, they are alien to us. Treat them carefully, like dealing with a campfire or similar. I was thinking of Freyja in my old talking to jesus buddy/buddy walk and style,I essentially tried to go hug a campfire.

In worse news my 3 year old was in the house when i did this. I'm terrified it may impact him in some way. After forcing the presence out I both prayed for my family to be left alone and also said via will and speaking out loud for them to be left alone and for all powers and entities to leave my house. That was more intuition though, was that appropriate in trying to ward them or does that even work that way?

I'd appreciate any advice you can give on what I should do next. At a minimum i'm halting all spiritual practice for the next week (divination, banishing, meditation) in hopes that the various energies from yesterday will subside. I will NOT be attempting seidh again, possibly ever. I have no idea how medium's do it, that stuff is terrifying. I feel pretty beat down at moment...

replies.title: Re: post part 2: in bad news, i asked to be ridden by Freyja via Seidh trance, it was terrifying
replies.content:
Okay, first, you realize you did something really stupid. That's a good start. Please next time do think about things like having a three year old in the house when you're invoking a deity!

Second, the last thing you want to do is stop banishing. Now's when you need do be doing your banishings at least every day, to clean your energy body and help you rebalance after the experience, and to dissipate the energies of the working. Stop everything else if you like, but keep banishing.

Third, Freyja gave you exactly what you asked for. If you still want to work with her -- and that may still be an option -- pray to her, and see what her reaction is. Trance possession is far from the only way to work with a deity; I've never done it -- my Golden Dawn obligations forbid me from making my mind and will subject to that of any other being -- and my religious life is fairly active and vivid. If you decide you can't handle working with her, or she decides she's not willing to work with you, well, them's the breaks.

comments.content:
Many thanks for Magic Mondays!

1) I've been wondering for sometime about, shall we saw, inadvertent Pathworkings. If you go to Burning Man, say, you may see a lot of people earnestly on the Path of Qoph, you go to med-school and there are probably quite a few people working on Shin. Personally, I experienced an awakening into the astral realm in my early 20's, with a lot of confirmed telepathy, prophetic dreams, and the ability to accurately define "vibes". These became just a normal part of my life. So, if I am understanding the concepts correctly I stumbled on to the path of Tau.

From my early 20's onward I've sought to "perfect everything under the Sun," with learning how to garden, herbalism, etc and passionately sharing my skills. This strikes me as very similar to the path of Tzaddi. Of course this process utterly transformed myself, on every level, according to my will, but in a manner very much "under the Sun". In that process, I learned to relate, but, importantly, not to individuate.

Am I on to something with this basic idea of "inadvertent Pathworkings" or am I missing something important?

2) In Paths of Wisdom, you write on page 40 that higher Spheres are always centripetal in relation to lower Spheres which are always centrifugal, so from the perspective of Tau, Yesod is uniting and potentially brings great strength, but from the perspective of Samech, Yesod brings dispersal and madness. This would imply that it is only safe to traverse the Tree in one direction alone; up!

Would that mean, then, that if someone had stumbled into and up the Tree they would be forced to continue ascent from wherever their at? If I have attained Netzachic consciousness but see that my ability to separate things in my mind are lacking, could I return to Yesod and then work the path of Resh to Hod? Or would I be limited to either the Paths of Nun or Kaph, with all different patterns of ascent from Malkuth permanently shut off?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You're always in all the Spheres, all the time. As long as you have a physical body you're in Malkuth, and so on. Thus people are always walking the Paths, inadvertently or otherwise, just as people are always practicing magic, consciously or otherwise.

Since you're always in all the Spheres, you don't need to "return" to Yesod; you're already there. Simply invoke Yesod and proceed up the Path of Resh from there. comments.title: sacrifice
comments.content:
Hello John and everybody.

In my recent reading on archaeological topics there have been many allusions to human sacrifice.
The Central and Southern American cultures are well known for it; also it was practised at certain times in Imperial China and Ireland, not to mention a couple of instances in the Bible, both by the Jews and by their enemies. the Greeks had their version, in Agamemnon's sacrifice of his daughter Iphigenia when setting off for the Trojan War... The extent is no doubt much greater than this.

It rather preoccupies me. Partly since it overlaps with a puzzling philosophical tenet that I have never resolved: namely, that while there may be such things as rights, they are a historically constrained concept and cannot be said to have always existed. Whereas we might feel strongly that what is good for you or me today is just as good for a person (which notion may also be historically mutable) three thousand years ago, it is not correct to make the same statements about each of us in terms of rights we bear. However, we may able to do the same work by stating alternatively that "it is wrong" for such-and-such to happen.

Well, I feel that human* sacrifice is wrong. Let's not go into why, for the moment. It's probably irrelevant. Perhaps we can consider one of the trains of thought that follows from the subject though.

Asking what purposes (human) sacrifice might further brings answers such as: thanks for success in a venture or process (eg conquest/harvest); prayers for success in like activities; maintenance of existential stability, as in continued seasonal cycles; prevention of chaos or disaster; straightforward statements of fealty to the assisting power. It seems to have been applied to situations pertinent at the societal level, although smaller groups or individuals doing it would not be unthinkable, just less likely to have left traces in the archaeological record.

Asking what kind of powers would like or require this kind of offering has generated more, and less straightforward, questions: what benefit would accrue to a creator power to have one of its creatures thus destroyed by others? (not much: the sacrifice is symbolic; what then is gratification worth?); what if the assisting power were not the creator of the victim?; what about deceitful entities claiming to be able to help and really taking more than they gave?; how often has the practice been simply part of elaborate mechanisms of social control by the elite?

And the pressing residue for me at this point is: (when) did it ever work? Would other devotional practices have succeeded as well? Did the deities involved move on up the chain of being or were their powers insufficient to keep the people going in the end? JMG, you mention in the Encyclopedia that many former magical practices are incompatible with current ecological values. So I infer that there are more than one way to achieve a result in natural magic... does the same go for these other kinds of practice?

People, I'd love your comments and insights.

kallianeira

* among other forms

replies.title: Re: sacrifice
replies.content: I've never really looked into the matter. I find the idea of human sacrifice repellent in the extreme, and so it's of zero interest to me, as my magical studies focus on those practices I'm interested in doing. One interesting historical tidbit: societies that routinely practice human sacrifice tend to crash and burn rather more dramatically than the run of the mill human society. I'm not sure if it's cause and effect, but that seems possible to me.

replies.content:
I've seen some anthropological/historical studies that indicate human sacrifice is common in societies of a certain size and level of complexity, and is dropped when the society gets larger or more complex. These studies indicate it's used as a method of social control by elites.

Most Jews and Christians are familiar with the story where Abraham goes to sacrifice his eldest (only?) son Isaac, and then he's switched for a stag (or ram or something). At the time it. was written, the message would have been very obvious: "God says: don't do this! The practice has been superseded."

John Roth

replies.title: Re: sacrifice
replies.content:
Christian apologist Gilbert Keith Chesterton had a fondness for teaching through paradoxical statements. In one essay I read long ago he said something to the effect that human sacrifice as an actual religious practice was superior to the way that modern industrial society devalued human life, because it recognized a human life as the most valuable thing that could be offered to the gods. Obvious, as a staunch Roman Catholic he was not advocating a return to what he would have seen as pagan barbarism. But he did have a point--for most societies that practiced it, human sacrifice was the ultimate effort--when herds of spotless bulls and rams were not enough to sway the gods, a virtuous and valuable human would be recruited. Where it starts to slip is when you don't get volunteers and start substituting prisoners of war, slaves or criminals.

If the gods who demanded or accepted human sacrifice were effective, one might expect that when human greed and the self-centerpieces of the ruling classes started offering them human discards instead of the cream of society they withdrew their support. One might also expect that gods that didn't ask for human victims might have more wholehearted and enthusiastic worshipers, whose societies might prosper as a result.

One way to look at this is to contrast the first film version of "The Wicker Man" with the novel. In the novel the policeman who is lured to the fake pagan island is a sincere, practicing Christian who resists the Summer Isle sexual rites because he is saving himself for marriage, not because he is, as in the film, a prude. When he realizes he is going to die in the sacrificial rite that he has been lured to, he devotes his final moments to freeing as many of the animal victims as possible, and devotes his soul in prayer to his own deity, Jesus. Any knowledgeable person reading that would recognize that Lord Summerisle has failed. The film, not-unexpectedly is deficient in any philosophical content.

I would hope that gods are as capable of learning as intelligent animals are, so even gods that go by the same names as they did in the past have probably reconciled themselves to hymns and incense, flowers and cookies instead of fresh barbecue.

comments.title: A Scientific Inquiry Into the Validity of Astrology
comments.content:
What do you think about this 1990 astrology study?

Abstract-Six expert astrologers independently attempted to match 23 astrological
birth charts to the corresponding case files of 4 male and 19
female volunteers. Case files contained information on the volunteers' life
histories, full-face and profile photographs, and test profiles from the
Strong-Campbell Vocational Interest Blank and the Cattell 16-P.F. Personality
Inventory. Astrologers did no better than chance or than a nonastrologer
control subject at matching the birth charts to the personal data; this
result was independent of astrologers' confidence ratings for their predicted
matches. Astrologers also failed to agree with one another's predictions.

http://www.skepticalmedia.com/astrology/Scientific%20Inquiry%20into%20Astrology.pdf

replies.title: Re: A Scientific Inquiry Into the Validity of Astrology
replies.content:
About what I'd think of any other study taken in isolation, without reference to the rest of the research on the subject, which has a very low sample size -- n = 6 isn't large enough to count even as a pilot study in most fields! -- which involves a task astrologers aren't trained to do, and which is presented by an organization that has a vested interest in the outcome and a well-earned reputation for fudging the facts.

Along the same lines, let's imagine that somebody presented a study that claimed to disprove the theory of evolution. The methodology involved taking six randomly chosen biologists and asking them to do something that isn't part of evolutionary biology. The study was presented to you by the Institute of Creation Science. How seriously would you take it?

replies.title: Re: A Scientific Inquiry Into the Validity of Astrology
replies.content:
I'm late to this discussion, but as far as we can see the study was honestly conducted [if not interpreted], so its results shouldn't be presumed fake because of who is promoting them (as scientism would do had they been positive). However, the conclusion that because the specified task is allegedly simpler than that of interpreting a whole birthchart, astrologers must surely be useless for the latter task as well, does not follow logically.

To start with a minor methodological issue, the authors should have demonstrated that the 23 test subjects were not similar in the chart characters that can vary within a single year (since all were 30 or 31 years old). They should have included a table of the birth dates, times and places so we could see that there weren't by chance, e.g., five native Hoosiers born in December and eight born at the time of day when American MDs like to do C-sections so they can get home to dinner.

Ultimately, the study methods don't make sense. I was going to suggest that using fewer subjects, chosen for their distinctly different birthplaces and times, could make differentiating them more feasible (instead of n=23, say, n=5). But this would simultaneously lead to charges of selection and risk random selection of outliers who would prevent any real effect from being seen. OTOH, if you greatly increased the number and age range of subjects to get more diversity - imagine n=1000 - with all the non-astrological influences on people's lives, astrologers couldn't possibly assign charts to individuals out of such a herd. Yet their failure would not be incompatible with a real effect of birth time on personality or life history. There's no group size for which we can say this task ought to be possible if there is a real effect, and therefore, the method is fundamentally inappropriate.

Which means, I suppose, that the Indiana astrologers were unwise to go along with it in the first place.

- Dewey

comments.title: A non witch/druid/wicca/orgone question
comments.content:
Abstract: I am currently prepared to tentatively accept the idea (formulated a hypothesis) that there are available methods and techniques that allow certain (all?) members of Homo Sapiens to observe and affect the world outside of the current understanding of physics (to go all Aristolian, these would be considered under the broad rubric of "metaphysics"). If this is so, is there a "reproducible" method one can use to observe the mechanism behind methods? I am quite uncomfortable with the idea of attempting to actively use the mechanism without studying it first.

John
(PS, the full post of this is abstracted from is here http://mightaswellliebackandenjoyit.blogspot.com/2018/07/sunday-morning-ii-have-go-at-this-one.html)

replies.title: Re: A non witch/druid/wicca/orgone question
replies.content:
Nope. This is one of those situations where we have no access to the mechanism; we don't even know if there is a mechanism separable from the methods in their historical, cultural, conceptual, and metaphysical contexts. All we have are arbitrary models that boil down to "when you do X, Y generally happens, and here's some handwaving to explain why."

The best source of guidance in such situations is history -- what has worked in the past? Some reading in the history of magic might be useful to you here.

First of all, a short presentation about myself.

I’m a (really rouge) “priest of Minerva’s Holy Church of Technology”. I mean that I’m an electronics engineer that works for a german transnational corporation that manufactures electronic products for automobiles, including Electric Vehicles, division where I had been an R&D engineer for some time.

The issue here that leads me to post today after so many years reading punctually these blogs with great joy, is the themes issued over here in the last months.

I began to study The Cosmic Doctrine, encouraged by this blog. My very first attempt at occult philosophy.

As a former Catholic Altar Boy, I expected a totally different kind of literature.

Instead, I found a mix of 3D design text book with some philosophy and electromagnetic concepts interwinned. Something that I’m used by my background. A fresh surprise to me, that makes such book really easy to read. Perhaps too much, though.

If we add the summer solstice astrology point, then my background kicked in fast, and I found a point that I want to share here.

As electronic engineer, with a good background in analog electronics (that’s because I’m old), I’m used to think about the universe in the frequency realm.

Let’s say that the tools that I’ve learned in my career let me understand the world by means of such toolkit, that includes ‘imaginary numbers’ (the square root of -1) and a totally different set of mathematical ‘gadgets’ that when applied to real things (in the mathematical sense), behave with a parts per million precision.

It is like some imaginary plane that can even be calculated manifests itself in the real plane by this, and the regular form of manifestation is frequency, cycles.

The standard tool to work out many of those analog approaches in the frequency domain is the Fourier Transform, another mathematical tool that also applies by imaginary numbers and that also only the real part is manifested, with a degree of accuracy really astonishing.

Such approach is ‘second nature’ to me, and is a huge part in the study of communication systems (I know that you also use radio), but also to the behavior of fed back systems, complex systems and system dynamics, like the Lotka-Volterra model.

The fancy part here, is that since this powerful tools that are commonly used to stabilize things like the flight of a Jumbo or a Harrier give such degree of accuracy and predictability, I wondered if that can be used also to ‘model’ other representations.

Since these are numerical tools, the best suited possibility for me was to apply it with a Big amount of processing power do a Big Data amount to finance, stock market, economical indexes and all related to ‘Juno’s Church’.

To my surprise, I found very few people that worked on that. Besides the Kondratieff cycles, other kind of observed economic cycles are even denied.

But, of course, at least one economist/programmer found a similar approach and it seems that it works perfectly for him. I refer to Martin Armstrong.

That is fully aligned with my concepts, and it seems that it is quite powerful, real, and applicable.

But if this can be applied to economy and finance, it may be also applicable to different realms too, like politics (‘Jupiter Maximus Church’), social issues, war, weather, etc.

Martin Armstrong is also doing the same, with a huge amount of processing power, data analysis, Artificial Intelligence, and the help of frequency or harmonic analysis tools. Fourier again.

Then, reading you, I’ve reached the same conclusion: Mundane Astrology is doing exactly the same. The correlations had been done by a big human effort by people like you, with some difficulty, and using the solar system as the Fourier Calculation Slide Rule.

And your explanations about how you calculate such things, interpret them, and explain them, fulfill my concept of Harmonic Analysis.

So, I think that I found a ‘bridge’ that relates ‘my former faith in technology’, and yours, and in between, something as different as economy is also linked to the same mental tool.

A tool that I consider a manifestation of other dimensions that we can’t reach, but we can ‘read’, ‘feel’ them.

With this in mind, I wonder if I have too much ‘Minerva’s background’ in my mind to disable me to follow the teachings in The Cosmic Doctrine, or even any occult doctrine at all.

Anyway, I will try hard to study it and meditate on the teaching. Meditation is a usual thing I do, but I’m heavily undisciplined at it, since non-linear thinking and ‘out of the box’ ideas are something I try to power, that give me good results in many fields, including my work.

Divination is something I’ve tried by I – Ching, but I worked on that only few weeks before I left its practice. Perhaps someday I will try again.

But the issue that kept me far from following your recommended practice was Rituals. Perhaps something related to elements (earth, fire, air, water) could be the best approach, but my absolute lack of knowledge in that field is a blocking point.

Where can I more info about that? Any advice?

Thanks for your writings and you insights, they are one of the best sources that cast light on the situation and reality.

Best regards,

Beamspot.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Got any advice for someone who once refused clairvoyance, or something like it, and is now ready to accept it?

I'd been a fairly intuitive kid and teenager. (Speaking of kids who are only loosely linked to the physical side of existence, my childhood imaginary friend was such a strong and widely liked presence that he became a neighborhood legend.) Then I grew to young adulthood, my knack got more serious, and I freaked out and shut down. Decades later, I'm ready to reopen those channels if they're willing to be reopened, preferably in a measured and temperate way. I'd like to know what advice you might have about that, and especially about any cautions you might have for me.

In my early twenties, I started outright knowing things I couldn't possibly know. It was always about an emotionally heightened situation that had happened or was just about to happen to someone I knew, and it'd come to me while I was woolgathering or puttering around. The knowledge didn't arrive with angelic harmonies or anything; it felt perfectly ordinary, aside from my feeling a little sheepish about it. (Occasionally there'd be a flash of what some people might call a vision in my mind's eye, but nothing more than you'd experience from a memory.) Often these were situations that anybody might expect -- young couples announcing a pregnancy, that sort of thing -- but sometimes they weren't, and the knowledge could be quite specific. After I foresaw my partner's parents winning a trip to Vegas in a contest I hadn't known they'd entered, I started to think that something might be going on beyond my imagination overheating.

So I tested it. The next time I had that particular feeling of sheepishness combined with a sense of sureness, I acted on it. I had the sense that I had an appointment with an acquaintance downtown, at a time and place when I definitely wouldn't have expected him. So downtown I went, and there he was, having something of an emergency. I was able to help him out and then we went on our respective ways, with me silently boggling.

JMG, I did not take this well. I was prepared to have my assumption confirmed that what was going on was a combination of coincidence and subliminal knowledge. I was sadly unprepared for the experiment to work.

I got spooked and flatly refused to have any more weird knowings. They stopped cold. I don't know what I did, but I sure did something. It was probably not a great idea.

Now that I'm in my forties, I'm less rigid about what's possible, and I've gotten used to the idea that the world is bigger than I'd dreamt of in my philosophy. I regret my refusal and I suspect that I overdid it. Strictly disallowing one's hunches turns out to be a recipe for foolishness, for one thing. For another thing, I would not be surprised to learn that it's not a coincidence that a few months later I started having a lot of mysterious and eventually overwhelming health problems, starting with severe multi-day migraines and getting rather significantly worse from there.

Plus, I can't shake the feeling that I've been a big ungrateful jerk about this.

I suspect the ability is still in there somewhere, and maybe not even all that latent. (I may have been a fool, but I've been a lucky fool.) In the last few years, I've had reason to suspect the block is crumbling on its own. My physical health has improved radically just as I've started to regain an ability to metaphorically see around corners. No inexplicable chunks of precise knowledge have fallen on my head, but I'm getting odd hunches again and those hunches are paying out. Occasionally it's uncomfortable: my chosen obliviousness did serve a protective function. All in all, it feels like a dam is cracking.

Plot twist: not long ago I was diagnosed with very minor temporal lobe epilepsy, aka "diviner's disease", after experiencing some apparently unrelated phenomena. It's not a huge deal, but it does encourage me not to take big risks with my brain. I would cheerfully put this whole thing down to a little TLE brain damage, but... I keep being right.

My question is broad: where do I go from here?

More particularly: could I have done myself a caution by refusing clairvoyance? If the block is coming down on its own, would I be well served to let it go naturally or is there a way to ease it down gently? Scanning the available literature on clairvoyance, such as it is-- well, is there anything you'd recommend that I read? I'd like not to be overwhelmed by intuitive information or go haring off after fabrications; how can I stay grounded? (Can gardening help? I'm a big fan of plants and rocks.) In general, how can I not be stupid about this?

Thanks.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'd encourage you to consider taking up meditation. Just a few minutes a day to begin with should be fine. The important thing is to get used to the process of relaxing and turning your attention back on itself, so you become aware of your awareness. Other than that, just let it come back naturally, and keep in mind the Three Druid Senses: common sense, a sense of proportion, and a sense of humor.

replies.title: Re: Egregore
replies.content:
I'm pretty sure I know which organization that is, for what it's worth. Once you have a tainted sphere -- that's the technical term for an egregor that's got toxic patterns built into it -- you can't save it; all you can do is shut it down, leave it completely dormant for seven years, and then try starting again with different symbolism and a formal act of atonement and renunciation of the corrupt patterns. If they even begin to surface again, then it's time to close down completely and disperse.

Since you don't have the capacity to shut it down, walking away is the best option. Go do something entirely different and you'll be fine.
